13 min read
Just now

--

ABLETON LIVE 12 · DIGITAL LOGICPREPRINT · V1
A STUDY IN UNCONVENTIONAL COMPUTING
Logic from Sound
A working computer, built entirely from Ableton Live’s stock devices — and made to play Snake.
Babak Ikani — independent research · audio-signal computation
ABSTRACTA commercial digital audio workstation is not meant to compute anything but sound. Yet its building blocks — an oscillator, a noise gate, a sidechain — are enough to build the two things a computer needs: a logic gate and a bit of memory. This paper documents a complete machine assembled from those parts inside Ableton Live 12, using no plug-ins and no code in the audio path. A single logic value is a 10 kHz tone (a 1) or silence (a 0); a single NOR gate is two stock Gate devices in series. From roughly five hundred of them I build flip-flops, an adder, a pseudo-random generator and a clock, and wire them into a hardwired implementation of Snake. I give the full construction, a small compiler that turns a gate netlist into a Live set, the several architectures considered along the way, and — at equal length — an honest account of what did not work: the analog degradation of deep gate chains, the timing of the read-out, and the practical ceiling of the audio engine. The conclusion is narrow but firm: Ableton Live is, in the formal sense, computationally universal.

1-The premise
Can a program made only of audio devices compute?
Every general-purpose computer, however large, reduces to two primitives: a device that performs Boolean logic, and a device that remembers a bit until the next tick of a clock. Given a logic gate that is functionally complete — one from which every other gate can be built — and a way to store state, one can, in principle, assemble any digital circuit at all. The question this project asks is whether a digital audio workstation, a tool designed for none of this, already contains both.
It does. The claim of this paper is constructive rather than theoretical: I do not argue that it could be done; I show the machine, running. The rest is a matter of representation — deciding what a 1 is, what a 0 is, and how the flow of audio through a chain of devices can be read as the flow of logic through a circuit.
A note on honesty, stated once and kept throughout. The computation below is entirely native: audio through stock devices. Where an external script is used — for a clock, for key presses, and to read the result onto a screen — it is named as such, and never confused with the machine itself. Those are the computer’s crystal, its keyboard, and its monitor; they are not the computer.

2-Representing a bit with sound
A 10 kHz tone is a 1. Silence is a 0.
The encoding is deliberately crude, because crude is robust. Logic-1 is the presence of a steady 10 kHz carrier, generated by an Operator set to a pure sine. Logic-0 is true digital silence. A single-frequency carrier well above the program material means a value can be detected by amplitude alone, and 10 kHz is high enough to sit clear of anything the logic itself produces.
A value is read from a track’s output meter: above a fixed threshold, the track carries a 1; at the floor, a 0. In practice a peak reading of the output_meter_level above 0.30 is treated as a one. The meter is linear in decibels, not in amplitude, so any arithmetic done on a reading must first be de-linearised against known reference levels — a subtlety that, left uncorrected, turns a true 0.5 into a reported 0.842 and every downstream claim into nonsense.
The signal is not a picture of the computation. The signal is the computation.

3-The universal gate
One NOR gate = two stock Gate devices, in series.
Ableton’s Gate is a noise gate: it lets a signal through only while a control input is loud enough, and is normally used to tighten a drum or duck a pad. Three of its parameters turn it into a logic element. Its sidechain is set to External, so the gate is keyed by another track rather than its own audio; its Floor is set to −∞ dB, so “closed” means genuine silence — a hard logic 0; and Flip is engaged, so the sense is inverted: when the keying input is loud, the gate shuts.
Feed such a gate a constant 10 kHz carrier and key it from input A. The output is the carrier when A is silent, and silence when A is present — that is NOT A. Put a second identical gate in series, keyed from input B, and the pair passes the carrier only when A and B are both silent. That is precisely NOR.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 1. One logic gate. Two Gate devices in series on a single audio track — External sidechain, Flip on, Floor at −∞ dB. Their two keying inputs are the gate’s inputs; the carrier that survives the pair is its output. Screenshot, Ableton Live 12.Press enter or click to view image in full size

NOR is one of the two functionally complete gates: NOT, AND, OR — and therefore every Boolean function — can be built from NOR alone. So a single, verified two-Gate track, cloned and re-wired, is all the logic hardware the entire machine will ever need. Every other component below is expressed in NOR, and nothing else:
HELPERS — NOR IS THE ONLY PRIMITIVE
# a gate is a track: (name, inputA, inputB) = NOR(inputA, inputB)
def NOT(o, a): return [(o, a, a)] # NOR(a,a) = NOT a
def OR (o, a, b): return [(o+"_n", a, b), (o, o+"_n", o+"_n")]
def AND(o, a, b): return [(o+"_na",a,a), (o+"_nb",b,b), (o, o+"_na", o+"_nb")]A comparator must not remember. The Gate’s Return parameter defaults to +3 dB of hysteresis — useful for a drum, fatal for a logic gate, because it makes the output depend on its own history. It is zeroed on every gate.

4-Memory, arithmetic, and time
From the one gate: a clock, a register, an adder, a random source.
A two-phase clock
Sequential logic needs a clock, and a single clock and its inverse will not do — the inverse arrives one gate late, and for those few milliseconds both halves of a flip-flop are open and the new value races through to the output. The fix is a pair of non-overlapping phases, p1 and p2, generated so that they are never high at once:
THE MACHINE’S CLOCK — SEVEN GATES
def clock():
return [("clk_n","CLK", "CLK"), # square up the raw pulse
("clk", "clk_n","clk_n"),
("nc", "clk", "clk"),
("p1", "clk", "p2"), # two non-overlapping phases
("p2", "nc", "p1"),
("np1", "p1", "p1"),
("np2", "p2", "p2")]A flip-flop
State is held in a master–slave D flip-flop — ten NOR gates, a master latch open only during p1 and a slave open only during p2, so a value is captured on one phase and presented on the next. Because every register in the machine shares the same two-phase clock, they all read each other’s old values and write their new ones together; this is what lets a bank of them behave as a shift register instead of collapsing to a single value.
An adder
Arithmetic is a ripple-carry full adder, nine NOR gates per bit, producing a sum and a carry. Movement on the grid is addition: to step the snake’s head, its position register is fed its own value plus an increment. Two’s-complement makes this economical — left is not a second, subtracting adder but simply “add 111”, which in three-bit arithmetic is minus one, and which overflows past seven back to zero exactly as the 8-cell grid wraps. The arithmetic and the geometry are the same fact.
A random source
The apple’s position is a six-bit linear-feedback shift register (LFSR): six flip-flops with an XOR of two taps fed back to the input. It is not truly random, but it is unpredictable enough to play against, and it costs one XOR and six flip-flops where a real entropy source would cost a great deal more and buy nothing a player could tell apart.
Every one of these — clock, flip-flop, adder, LFSR, counter — is written purely in the NOR helper of §3. There is no other primitive anywhere in the design.

5-A compiler, from netlist to Live set
Hand-wiring five hundred sidechains is not an option.
The circuit is described as a plain netlist — a Python list of (name, inputA, inputB) triples, each one a NOR gate. A small compiler, norc, turns that list into a finished .als project. It reads a template set containing one known-good, hand-verified NOR track and one carrier track, then, for every gate in the netlist, clones the verified track, gives it a fresh internal id-space so no two clones collide, renames it, and rewrites its two sidechain sources to point at the named inputs. A single always-on carrier rail — VCC — feeds every gate.
NORC.PY — THE CORE OF THE COMPILER (ABRIDGED)
def compile_circuit(src_als, out_als, circuit, audio_inputs=()):
q_tpl = extract_block(xml, "MidiTrack", 24) # the ONE verified NOR gate
names = ["VCC"] + circuit["inputs"] + [g[0] for g in circuit["gates"]]
tid = {n: 100+i for i,n in enumerate(names)} # fresh track ids for name, a, b in circuit["gates"]:
blk = clone(q_tpl); blk = remap_ids(blk) # disjoint id space
blk = set_audio_input(blk, tid["VCC"]) # carrier rail in
blk = set_sidechains (blk, [(tid[a],a),(tid[b],b)]) # wire the two inputs
blocks.append(set_track(blk, tid[name], name))
# ... splice the compiled <Tracks> body back into the .als and gzip it
An input named in audio_inputs becomes an audio track rather than an instrument track, so it can play a WAV — a necessity, not a nicety: an Operator can be switched on but never truly off (its note-offs never reach the amplifier), whereas a clock must go low and a key must be released. The result is a project of a few hundred to a thousand tracks, every one a gate, that opens and runs like any other Live set.

6-Architectures considered
Several ways to build the same machine — and why the flat one won.
The design above is a flat parallel gate array: one physical track per gate, all live at once. It is the most transparent — every wire in the schematic is a track you can point at — but it is also the largest, and its size sets the practical ceiling discussed in §9. Two alternatives were weighed.
Bit-serial with delay-line memory
The earliest electronic computers (EDSAC, UNIVAC I, ~1951) did not give every bit its own hardware. They stored a word as a train of pulses circulating in a delay line and processed it one bit at a time. Ableton has a native delay line — the Delay device — and a native way to hold a value indefinitely — Freeze. A bit-serial machine built this way trades hundreds of parallel tracks for a handful of recirculating memory tracks plus a small serial datapath; a plausible reimplementation fits in roughly forty tracks rather than five hundred. It is dramatically smaller, but it is also far more sensitive to timing: a serial machine lives or dies by its clock, and the clock, as §9 shows, is the fragile part. It was left as future work.
Analog arithmetic
Not everything must be digital. A Utility device is a signed gain; a return track is a zero-latency summing node; and a Gate used as a bare comparator computes a min(). A whole class of problems — anything expressible as repeated add a step to a position — can be built directly in analog, with a fraction of the devices, by letting an 8-bit accumulator march. These analog methods are exact and cheap but special-purpose; they do not give you a programmable machine, and Snake needs branching, memory and randomness, which are digital. The digital array was therefore the right substrate for a game, and the analog methods were kept for the parts (a smooth counter, a comparison) where they were strictly cheaper.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

7-Snake, as a hardwired program
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The head is one axis of arithmetic, and the whole of it is this:
SNAKE — THE HEAD IS AN ADDER THAT ADDS ITS OWN DIRECTION
def axis(name, plus, minus): # next = self + inc
g = OR(name+"_i0", plus, minus) # inc bit0: set either way
inc = [name+"_i0", minus, minus] # 001 = +1 · 111 = −1 · 000 = hold
carry = "GND"
for k in range(3):
g += full_adder(...); g += dff(...) # adder feeds a flip-flop, and loops
ret
